news_summary: Today's space news highlights advancements in human research methodologies for lunar missions, innovative citizen contributions to detecting rare space weather phenomena, and renewed interest in evolving cosmological theories.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant les progrès réalisés dans les méthodes de recherche humaine pour les missions lunaires, les contributions innovantes des citoyens à la détection de phénomènes météorologiques spatiaux rares, ainsi que le regain d'intérêt pour les théories cosmologiques en constante évolution.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan los avances en las metodologías de investigación con tripulaciones para las misiones lunares, las innovadoras contribuciones de los ciudadanos a la detección de fenómenos meteorológicos espaciales poco comunes y el renovado interés por las teorías cosmológicas en constante evolución.

articles.title: NASA Artemis II Human Research Data Methodology Challenge
articles.summary: NASA’s Human Research Program (HRP) uses research to develop methods to protect the health and performance of astronauts in space. In support of NASA’s goals for long-term missions on the surface of the Moon and human exploration of Mars, HRP is using ground research facilities, the International Space Station, and analog environments to monitor human health in deep space.  NASA’s Artemis II mission was the first […]
articles.content:
art002e013365 (April 7, 2026) – The Artemis II crew – (clockwise from left) Mission Specialist Christina Koch, Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen, Commander Reid Wiseman, and Pilot Victor Glover – pause for a group photo with their zero gravity indicator “Rise,” inside the Orion spacecraft on their way home. Following a swing around the far side of the Moon on April 6, 2026, the crew exited the lunar sphere of influence (the point at which the Moon’s gravity has a stronger pull on Orion than the Earth’s) on April 7, and are headed back to Earth for a splashdown in the Pacific Ocean on April 10.

NASA’s Human Research Program (HRP) uses research to develop methods to protect the health and performance of astronauts in space. In support of NASA’s goals for long-term missions on the surface of the Moon and human exploration of Mars, HRP is using ground research facilities, the International Space Station, and analog environments to monitor human health in deep space.

NASA’s Artemis II mission was the first crewed mission to the vicinity of the Moon since Apollo 17 in December 1972. The mission carried four astronauts aboard the Orion spacecraft on a trajectory into deep space – farther than any humans have gone before – marking a pivotal milestone in the history of human exploration. For the first time in more than half a century, human beings experienced the full physiological and psychological conditions of space travel beyond low Earth orbit, including an environment with space radiation, the isolation and confinement of a new spacecraft, and the operational demands of a test mission profile.

For HRP, Artemis II represents an irreplaceable research opportunity. The data collected from the four-person crew will expand an existing body of knowledge built primarily from missions in low Earth orbit, extending it into the deep space environment. It will provide direct measurements of how the human body responds to conditions that ground-based simulation cannot fully replicate.

The unique dataset will also present a profound analytical challenge. Though the sample size is only four subjects, the data will span multiple physiological systems, data modalities, and time points. That combination is what the NASA Artemis II Human Research Data Methodology Challenge seeks to address.

Award: $25,000 in total prizes

Challenge Open Date: March 30, 2026

Submission Close Date: June 5, 2026

For more information, visit: https://hrpdatachallenge.org/
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's Artemis II mission, the first crewed mission beyond low Earth orbit since 1972, which provides critical human health data in deep space. This represents a significant milestone in human space exploration with broad implications for future long-term missions to the Moon and Mars. The research has tangible benefits for astronaut health and performance, advancing scientific understanding with a clear positive real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Artemis II mission advances human health research for deep space travel
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is preparing for long missions to the Moon and Mars. To keep astronauts healthy during these trips, NASA’s Human Research Program (HRP) studies how space affects the body and mind. They use labs on Earth, the International Space Station, and special environments that simulate space conditions.

**What happened** – In April 2026, NASA’s Artemis II mission sent four astronauts farther into space than anyone has gone since 1972. The crew traveled around the Moon and collected important health data while experiencing real deep space conditions like radiation and isolation. This was the first time humans faced these conditions beyond low Earth orbit in over 50 years.

**Impact** – The health information gathered from this small group is very valuable because it shows how the human body reacts to deep space, something ground tests can’t fully copy. This data helps scientists learn what astronauts will need to stay safe and strong on future long trips to the Moon and Mars.

**What’s next step** – NASA has launched a challenge inviting researchers to develop new ways to analyze this complex data from Artemis II. The challenge offers $25,000 in prizes and runs until June 5, 2026. This will help unlock insights from the mission and improve space health research.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Artemis II mission provides unique health data from deep space that will improve safety and performance for future Moon and Mars explorers.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Artemis II de la NASA fait progresser la recherche sur la santé humaine en espace lointain
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA prépare de longues missions vers la Lune et Mars. Pour protéger la santé des astronautes pendant ces voyages, le Programme de Recherche Humaine (HRP) étudie comment l’espace affecte le corps et l’esprit. Ils utilisent des laboratoires sur Terre, la Station Spatiale Internationale, et des environnements qui simulent l’espace.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En avril 2026, la mission Artemis II de la NASA a envoyé quatre astronautes plus loin dans l’espace que jamais depuis 1972. L’équipage a fait le tour de la Lune et collecté des données importantes sur la santé en vivant les vraies conditions de l’espace profond, comme les radiations et l’isolement. C’était la première fois en plus de 50 ans que des humains expérimentaient ces conditions au-delà de l’orbite basse.

**Impact** – Les informations de santé recueillies, bien que venant d’un petit groupe, sont précieuses car elles montrent comment le corps humain réagit à l’espace profond, ce que les tests au sol ne peuvent pas reproduire complètement. Ces données aident les scientifiques à comprendre ce dont les astronautes auront besoin pour rester en forme lors de futurs voyages longs vers la Lune et Mars.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA a lancé un défi invitant les chercheurs à développer de nouvelles méthodes pour analyser ces données complexes d’Artemis II. Ce défi offre 25 000 $ de prix et se termine le 5 juin 2026. Cela aidera à mieux comprendre la mission et à améliorer la recherche sur la santé en espace.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA fournit des données uniques sur la santé en espace profond qui amélioreront la sécurité et la performance des futurs explorateurs de la Lune et de Mars.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Artemis II de la NASA impulsa la investigación sobre la salud humana en viajes espaciales profundos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA se prepara para misiones largas a la Luna y Marte. Para proteger la salud de los astronautas durante estos viajes, el Programa de Investigación Humana (HRP) estudia cómo el espacio afecta el cuerpo y la mente. Usan laboratorios en la Tierra, la Estación Espacial Internacional y ambientes que simulan el espacio.

**Qué pasó** – En abril de 2026, la misión Artemis II de la NASA llevó a cuatro astronautas más lejos en el espacio que nadie desde 1972. La tripulación orbitó la Luna y recolectó datos importantes de salud mientras vivían las condiciones reales del espacio profundo, como la radiación y el aislamiento. Fue la primera vez en más de 50 años que humanos experimentaron estas condiciones fuera de la órbita baja.

**Impacto** – La información de salud recolectada, aunque de un grupo pequeño, es muy valiosa porque muestra cómo reacciona el cuerpo humano al espacio profundo, algo que las pruebas en tierra no pueden replicar completamente. Estos datos ayudan a los científicos a saber qué necesitan los astronautas para mantenerse saludables en futuros viajes largos a la Luna y Marte.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA ha lanzado un desafío para que investigadores desarrollen nuevas formas de analizar estos datos complejos de Artemis II. El desafío ofrece 25,000 dólares en premios y cierra el 5 de junio de 2026. Esto ayudará a descubrir más sobre la misión y a mejorar la investigación en salud espacial.

**Una frase clave** – La misión Artemis II de la NASA entrega datos únicos sobre la salud en el espacio profundo que mejorarán la seguridad y el rendimiento de futuros exploradores de la Luna y Marte.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sleek, stylized spacecraft orbiting a softly glowing crescent Moon, with four abstract, interconnected orbits representing the Artemis II crew’s physiological data streams weaving around the ship, set against a calm, deep space background with gentle earth and ocean hues hinting at the journey home.

articles.title: Volunteers Discover Rare Space Weather Events Using Their Ears
articles.summary: Scientists are working to understand exactly how these waves behave, and the team behind NASA’s Heliophysics Audified: Resonances in Plasmas (HARP) citizen science project approaches this in a unique way: they compare the Earth’s magnetic field to a giant harp in space.
articles.content:
Our planet rests inside a magnetic cocoon filled with plasma – but it’s not always peaceful and quiet. Activity from the Sun can send waves through this space, and some of those disturbances can even reach Earth, affecting our power grid.

Scientists are working to understand exactly how these waves behave, and the team behind NASA’s Heliophysics Audified: Resonances in Plasmas (HARP) citizen science project approaches this in a unique way: they compare the Earth’s magnetic field to a giant harp in space. The HARP team translated magnetic field measurements into sound. This translation allowed HARP project volunteers to use their ears to study a particular type of plasma wave that plays a role in space weather. What they heard surprised everyone.

The science team expected lower pitches farther from Earth and higher pitches closer to it. But when they played back data from NASA’s THEMIS (Time History of Events and Macroscale Interactions during Substorms) mission, volunteers noticed something unexpected. Some plasma waves revealed the opposite pattern – lower pitches close to Earth and higher pitches farther away.

The HARP volunteers were thrilled to help discover this anomaly, which will help scientists better understand geomagnetic storms. One volunteer said of the HARP project, “I only signed up for this group because my friend was participating, but now I think I’m going to change my major to physics – this was just too cool.” These findings now appear in a new article in Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences.

Thank you to all the HARP volunteers who helped develop the project’s audio analysis protocol, beta tested the graphical user interface, and identified and labeled the myriad plasma waves that the team will be studying for years to come.

The HARP project was sponsored by NASA and continues to be sponsored by the National Science Foundation. The project is no longer actively seeking volunteers.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a unique citizen science project where volunteers helped discover unexpected plasma wave patterns affecting space weather, contributing to scientific understanding with broad implications for Earth's power grid and space weather prediction.
articles.personality_title: Volunteers use sound to uncover surprising space weather patterns
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Earth is surrounded by a magnetic field filled with plasma, a charged gas affected by waves from the Sun. These waves can sometimes disturb our planet’s magnetic environment and impact things like power grids.

**What happened** – NASA’s Heliophysics Audified: Resonances in Plasmas (HARP) project turned magnetic field data into sounds so volunteers could listen to plasma waves in space. While analyzing sounds from NASA’s THEMIS mission, volunteers noticed unexpected patterns: waves closer to Earth had lower sounds, opposite to what scientists predicted.

**Impact** – This unusual discovery helps scientists better understand how plasma waves behave during space storms. Knowing this can improve predictions of geomagnetic storms that might affect technology on Earth. The project also inspired some volunteers to take a new interest in science.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying these plasma waves using the volunteers’ findings. The new knowledge could lead to improved space weather forecasts and more protection for Earth's power systems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Citizen volunteers listening to space sounds helped reveal surprising plasma wave behavior, advancing our understanding of space weather effects on Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des bénévoles utilisent le son pour découvrir des phénomènes surprenants dans la météo spatiale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Terre est entourée d’un champ magnétique rempli de plasma, un gaz chargé affecté par des ondes venues du Soleil. Ces ondes peuvent parfois perturber l’environnement magnétique de notre planète et toucher des infrastructures comme les réseaux électriques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le projet HARP de la NASA a transformé les données du champ magnétique en sons pour que des bénévoles puissent écouter les ondes de plasma dans l’espace. En analysant les sons de la mission THEMIS, ils ont remarqué des modèles inattendus : les ondes proches de la Terre avaient des sons plus graves, à l’inverse des prévisions des scientifiques.

**Impact** – Cette découverte aide les chercheurs à mieux comprendre le comportement des ondes de plasma lors des tempêtes spatiales. Cela peut améliorer la prévision des tempêtes géomagnétiques qui peuvent affecter nos technologies. Le projet a aussi donné envie à certains bénévoles de s’intéresser davantage aux sciences.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à étudier ces ondes en s’appuyant sur les découvertes des bénévoles. Ces nouvelles connaissances pourraient permettre de mieux prévoir la météo spatiale et de mieux protéger les réseaux électriques.

**Phrase clé** – Des bénévoles écoutant les sons de l’espace ont aidé à révéler un comportement surprenant des ondes de plasma, faisant avancer notre compréhension des effets de la météo spatiale sur Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Voluntarios usan el sonido para descubrir patrones sorprendentes en el clima espacial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Tierra está rodeada por un campo magnético lleno de plasma, un gas cargado que es afectado por ondas del Sol. Estas ondas a veces pueden alterar el entorno magnético de nuestro planeta y afectar infraestructuras como las redes eléctricas.

**Qué pasó** – El proyecto HARP de la NASA convirtió datos del campo magnético en sonidos para que voluntarios pudieran escuchar las ondas de plasma en el espacio. Al analizar sonidos de la misión THEMIS, notaron patrones inesperados: las ondas cerca de la Tierra tenían sonidos más graves, contrario a lo que los científicos esperaban.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo se comportan las ondas de plasma durante tormentas espaciales. Esto puede mejorar las predicciones de tormentas geomagnéticas que afectan la tecnología en la Tierra. El proyecto también motivó a algunos voluntarios a interesarse más en la ciencia.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando estas ondas usando los resultados de los voluntarios. Este nuevo conocimiento podría mejorar los pronósticos del clima espacial y proteger mejor los sistemas eléctricos.

**Frase clave** – Voluntarios que escucharon sonidos del espacio ayudaron a descubrir un comportamiento sorprendente de las ondas de plasma, avanzando en nuestra comprensión de los efectos del clima espacial en la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a giant cosmic harp made of glowing magnetic field lines stretching across a starry space backdrop, with delicate plasma waves depicted as colorful sound waves flowing toward a stylized Earth silhouette wrapped in a soft magnetic cocoon, all rendered in gentle natural hues.

articles.title: The rise, the fall and the rebound of cyclic cosmology
articles.summary: Cyclic cosmology, or the big bounce, is the idea that the universe will eventually crunch back together and then go through another big bang. Columnist Leah Crane finds that, appropriately, it’s coming back
articles.content:
Cyclic cosmology, or the big bounce, is the idea that the universe will eventually crunch back together and then go through another big bang. Columnist Leah Crane finds that, appropriately, it’s coming back

The largest 3D map of our universe to date, with Earth at the center and every dot showing a galaxy DESI collaboration and KPNO/NOIRLab/NSF/AURA/R. Proctor

The universe is dead; long live the universe.

Not right at this moment, not yet. But one day everything we know will be gone. The cities we build, the lakes we swim in, the planet we live on, the solar system we inhabit, the star we orbit and every star we don’t – they’re all headed towards an inescapable finale.

At the end of it all, what happens? Some say our ever-expanding universe will slow down and then one day do a cosmic U-turn, undoing all the growth that has happened since the big bang. Eventually, everything will crunch together into the tiniest possible space and then explode out again in a riot of rebirth – that’s the idea we call cyclic cosmology, or the big bounce. It’s been around for a long time, and the idea itself has faced a trajectory that mirrors its contents. It was briefly popular in the mid-20th century, fell from favour, and now it may be making a comeback thanks to new data from the largest 3D map of the universe ever created, made by the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI).

As is occasionally the case with grand cosmological hypotheses, proponents of cyclic cosmology mostly preferred it for its elegance: if the universe is cyclic, that means we probably don’t have to worry about what precipitated the big bang or what existed before it – those near-impossible questions are already answered. There’s a beautiful sense of symmetry to the whole thing. Catherine Heymans, the Astronomer Royal for Scotland, expressed it nicely during a recent New Scientist subscriber event I hosted, where she said, “It really gels with me that the universe sort of is created in a big bang, it expands, it slows down, gravity pulls it back in on itself, there’s a big crunch, there’s another big bang and it expands… This just makes me very happy.”

At the same event, Adam Riess, who won a Nobel for his and his colleagues’ discovery of dark energy, put forward one of the more concrete reasons for many cosmologists’ fondness for the idea. “We like it because it tells us that this is not a special time that we live in or the one-shot universe,” he said. In other words, in a cyclic universe it wouldn’t be quite such an unbelievable coincidence that we’re here at all to ponder these things. Personally, I don’t think the idea that times like this happen over and over again – perhaps not in every bounce but definitely in more than one – with all the right conditions for life and trees and rockets to the moon, makes it that much less special, but I digress. Maybe that’s more of an anthropocentric, emotional position than one based on the laws of physics.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

For a long time, cyclic cosmology fell out of favour, driven partly by Riess’s work showing that the universe is expanding at an accelerating rate. If the space between the stars is growing faster and faster, it feels unlikely that it will eventually shrink back down to nothing again. Gravity just isn’t strong enough to counteract dark energy. “Unfortunately, all of the measurements that we make tell us that there just isn’t enough mass in the universe to pull it back together,” said Heymans. “At the moment, the evidence is pointing towards a very cold and sad and empty death for our universe.” This idea, called the heat death, is now the most accepted version of what is to come.

There are various other reasons that the big bounce faded into relative obscurity, largely to do with problems that arise when we try to sort out how matter, energy and entropy might be recycled or destroyed in the moment between bounces.

The second law of thermodynamics is a sticking point: it says that disorder, or entropy, in a closed system (such as the universe, as far as we know) can never decrease. With an expanding universe, that’s easy to square – we would just see a continual slow increase in entropy over the lifetime of the cosmos. But if the universe starts contracting again, entropy would correspondingly start to decrease. There are ways around this, generally involving pushing the problem off into the next cycle of expansion and contraction. If the universe gets bigger in each cycle, entropy is still increasing overall. But if you extrapolate backwards or forwards in time enough, you end up in the same situation as before. We still start with a big bang at the beginning of the universe and an eventual heat death at the end, it’s just a more complicated, stepwise path between the two.

Another way around the entropy problem was popularised in the 2010s by legendary theoretical physicist Roger Penrose, of Penrose triangle fame. His model is called conformal cyclic cosmology, and it would look exactly like an ever-expanding universe… right until the very end. As the universe expands and everything gets further and further from everything else, matter will decay into its composite parts, and eventually everything will just be leftover photons floating in the abyss. That’s not particularly controversial. But what Penrose proposed next is. His idea is that the extreme emptiness and uniformity of space-time at the end of one cycle, or aeon, is the same as the structure we’d expect at the very beginning of a new aeon. The idea behind conformal cyclic cosmology is that thanks to this functionally identical structure (and some very complicated maths), a new, expanding universe can be kicked off from the frigid remains of the previous one.

The idea is niche and difficult (bordering on impossible) to test. Penrose has proposed some potentially measurable bits of evidence for it, but on the whole, cosmologists tend to find them unconvincing. However, it hasn’t been disproven either, and the fact that it manages to get around the entropy problem means that it shouldn’t be simply discarded, even if it is widely viewed with scepticism. So, we’re stuck without much of a way to apply these ideas to the real universe we live in.

The Mayall 4-meter Telescope at Kitt Peak National Observatory, which is used by DESI to survey the stars DESI Collaboration/DOE/KPNO/NOIR

Enter DESI. Its enormous map of the universe has shown that dark energy, which previously looked like it would only grow in strength forever, seems to be weakening. That is, the outward acceleration of the universe appears to be slowing. As Heymans stressed during the event, this does not mean that the universe is coming back together – it’s still accelerating in its expansion, just not quite as quickly. Still, this is a radical shift in our understanding of dark energy, and it could well kick off an era of new theories about the way our cosmos will spend its final days.

And among those new theories, cyclic cosmologies seem to be rising once again. “What could be causing dark energy to change could mean that in another 10 billion years’ time, dark energy weakens so much that it does reverse and it does pull everything back in on itself, which would be lovely,” said Heymans.

The problem with knowing what it all means is that we don’t understand a massive proportion of the universe. Dark energy makes up nearly 70 per cent of all the matter and energy in the entire cosmos; it controls the ultimate fate of everything, and yet we have no idea what it is or how it works. On cosmological time scales, we just met – Reiss and his colleagues only identified it less than 30 years ago.

“Without understanding the nature of the dark energy that’s driving the present acceleration, it’s very difficult to extrapolate it into the future. Will it weaken?” said Riess. “I would say all bets are off about the future.” Smart money may still be on a cold and empty end of the universe, but, for the first time in a century, it might also be worth placing a long-shot wager on the big bounce.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses a significant scientific development in cosmology: new data suggesting dark energy may be weakening, potentially reviving the cyclic cosmology theory. This has broad implications for our understanding of the universe's fate, inspiring curiosity and hope about cosmic rebirth rather than a cold, empty end. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful shift in scientific perspective with large-scale impact on humanity's view of the cosmos.
articles.personality_title: New universe map hints dark energy may be weakening, reviving big bounce theory
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study how the universe began and how it might end. One idea, called cyclic cosmology or the big bounce, suggests the universe expands, then shrinks, and then expands again in a never-ending cycle.

**What happened** – The Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) created the largest 3D map of the universe. This map shows that dark energy, which makes the universe expand faster, might be slowing down. Because of this, some scientists think the universe might eventually stop expanding and start shrinking, supporting the big bounce idea.

**Impact** – This is important because for many years, scientists believed the universe would keep expanding forever, ending in a cold, empty space. Now, the new data opens the possibility that the universe could one day collapse and start over. This changes how we think about the universe’s future and the big bang’s place in a larger cycle.

**What's next step** – Researchers will study dark energy more to understand if it really is weakening. Since dark energy is still a mystery, learning more about it could confirm or reject the big bounce theory. Future observations and experiments will help scientists predict the universe’s fate better.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New cosmic data suggests dark energy may be slowing, making the idea of a universe that cycles through big bangs and crunches possible again.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle carte de l’univers suggère un affaiblissement de l’énergie sombre, relançant la théorie du grand rebond
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient comment l’univers a commencé et comment il pourrait finir. Une idée, appelée cosmologie cyclique ou grand rebond, suggère que l’univers s’étend, puis se contracte, puis s’étend à nouveau dans un cycle sans fin.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’instrument spectroscopique d’énergie sombre (DESI) a créé la plus grande carte 3D de l’univers. Cette carte montre que l’énergie sombre, qui accélère l’expansion de l’univers, pourrait ralentir. À cause de cela, certains scientifiques pensent que l’univers pourrait un jour arrêter de s’étendre et commencer à se contracter, ce qui soutient l’idée du grand rebond.

**Impact** – Cela est important car pendant longtemps, les scientifiques pensaient que l’univers continuerait à s’étendre pour toujours, aboutissant à un espace froid et vide. Maintenant, les nouvelles données ouvrent la possibilité que l’univers s’effondre un jour et recommence à zéro. Cela change notre façon de penser à l’avenir de l’univers et à la place du big bang dans un cycle plus large.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier davantage l’énergie sombre pour comprendre si elle s’affaiblit vraiment. Comme l’énergie sombre est encore un mystère, mieux la connaître pourrait confirmer ou infirmer la théorie du grand rebond. Les futures observations et expériences aideront les scientifiques à mieux prédire le destin de l’univers.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouvelles données cosmiques suggèrent que l’énergie sombre ralentit, rendant à nouveau possible l’idée d’un univers qui passe par des big bangs et des effondrements cycliques.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo mapa del universo sugiere que la energía oscura podría estar debilitándose, reavivando la teoría del gran rebote
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian cómo comenzó el universo y cómo podría terminar. Una idea llamada cosmología cíclica o gran rebote dice que el universo se expande, luego se contrae y luego se expande otra vez en un ciclo sin fin.

**Qué pasó** – El Instrumento Espectroscópico de Energía Oscura (DESI) creó el mapa 3D más grande del universo. Este mapa muestra que la energía oscura, que hace que el universo se expanda más rápido, podría estar desacelerándose. Por eso, algunos científicos piensan que el universo podría dejar de expandirse y empezar a contraerse, apoyando la idea del gran rebote.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque durante mucho tiempo, los científicos creyeron que el universo se expandiría para siempre, terminando en un espacio frío y vacío. Ahora, los nuevos datos abren la posibilidad de que el universo un día se colapse y comience de nuevo. Esto cambia cómo pensamos sobre el futuro del universo y el lugar del big bang en un ciclo mayor.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores estudiarán más la energía oscura para entender si realmente está debilitándose. Como la energía oscura sigue siendo un misterio, aprender más podría confirmar o rechazar la teoría del gran rebote. Observaciones y experimentos futuros ayudarán a los científicos a predecir mejor el destino del universo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Nuevos datos cósmicos sugieren que la energía oscura podría estar desacelerándose, haciendo posible otra vez la idea de un universo que pasa por big bangs y colapsos en ciclos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast cosmic cycle represented by two intertwined glowing spheres—one contracting with swirling galaxies and stars pulled inward, the other expanding outward with vibrant bursts of light and colorful nebulae—set against a deep, softly textured space background in natural blues, purples, and golds, symbolizing the universe’s eternal rhythm of big crunch and big bang without human figures or text.
